body_text:
Section 5.1 Cauchy's Residue Theorem

To extract the residue $a_{-1}$ out of this expansion, first multiply by $\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{m}$ :
$$
\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{m} f(z)=a_{-m}+\cdots+a_{-1}\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{m-1}+a_{0}\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{m}+a_{1}\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{m+1}+
$$

Then differentiate $(m-1)$ times to obtain
$$
\frac{d^{m-1}}{d z^{m-1}}\left[\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{m} f(z)\right]=(m-1)!a_{-1}+m!a_{0}\left(z-z_{0}\right)+\frac{(m+1)!}{2} a_{1}\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{2}+
$$

Finally, take the limit as $z \rightarrow z_{0}$, and get
$$
\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}} \frac{d^{m-1}}{d z^{m-1}}\left[\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{m} f(z)\right]=(m-1)!a_{-1}+0
$$
which is equivalent to (9).
EXAMPLE 2 Computing residues
Let $C$ be the simple closed path shown in Figure 3. (a) Compute the residue $f(z)=\frac{z^{2}}{\left(z^{2}+\pi^{2}\right)^{2} \sin z}$ at all the isolated singularities inside $C$. (b) Evaluate
$$
\int_{C} \frac{z^{2}}{\left(z^{2}+\pi^{2}\right)^{2} \sin z} d z
$$

Solution (a) Three steps are involved in answering this question.
Step 1: Determine the singularities of $f$ inside $C$. The function $f(z)=\frac{z^{2}}{\left(z^{2}+\pi^{2}\right)^{2}}$ is analytic except where $z^{2}+\pi^{2}=0$ or $\sin z=0$. Thus $f$ has isolated singulari at $\pm i \pi$ and at $k \pi$ where $k$ is an integer. Only 0 and $i \pi$ are inside $C$.
Step 2: Determine the type of the singularities of $f$ inside $C$. Let us start the singularity at 0 . Using $\lim _{z \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin z}{z}=1$, it follows that $\lim _{z \rightarrow 0} \frac{z}{\sin z}=1$, an
$$
\lim _{z \rightarrow 0} f(z)=\lim _{z \rightarrow 0} \frac{z}{\sin z} \frac{z}{\left(z^{2}+\pi^{2}\right)^{2}}=1 \cdot 0=0
$$

By Theorem 5, Section 4.6, $f(z)$ has a removable singularity at $z_{0}=0$. To t, the singularities at $i \pi$, we consider $\frac{1}{f(z)}=\frac{(z+i \pi)^{2}(z-i \pi)^{2} \sin z}{z^{2}}$. Clearly $\frac{1}{f(z)}$ h $z$ zero of order 2 at $i \pi$, and so by Theorem 7(v), Section 4.6, $f(z)$ has a pole of or 2 at $i \pi$.
Step 3: Determine the residues of $f$ inside $C$. At $0, f$ has a removable singula which means that its Laurent series expansion around zero has no terms with ative powers of $z$. In particular, $a_{-1}=0$, and so the residue of $f$ at a remove singularity is 0 . At $i \pi$, we can apply Theorem 2, with $m=2, z_{0}=i \pi$. Then
$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Res}(i \pi) & =\lim _{z \rightarrow i \pi} \frac{d}{d z}\left[(z-i \pi)^{2} f(z)\right] \\
& =\lim _{z \rightarrow i \pi} \frac{d}{d z}\left[\frac{z^{2}}{(z+i \pi)^{2} \sin z}\right] \\
& =\lim _{z \rightarrow i \pi} \frac{2 z(z+i \pi) \sin z-z^{2}((z+i \pi) \cos z+2 \sin z)}{(z+i \pi)^{3} \sin ^{2} z} \\
& =\frac{2 \sinh \pi+(-\pi \cosh \pi-\sinh \pi)}{-4 \pi \sinh ^{2} \pi}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \sinh \pi}+\frac{\cosh \pi}{4 \pi \sinh ^{2} \pi}
\end{aligned}
$$
